name: Guyanese Bakes and Saltfish

ingredients.note: well-salted, or true saltfish if available

steps: Season the fish well with extra salt (mimicking salted, dried fish) and pan-fry or poach until cooked through, then flake it.
Mix the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, and water into a soft dough, then flatten into small rounds.
Fry the dough rounds until puffed and golden, and serve alongside the flaked fish.

ingredients_full.note: well-salted, or true saltfish if available

steps_detailed: Salt the fish generously and let it sit 10-15 minutes to firm up and concentrate flavor, mimicking traditional dried saltfish.
Poach or pan-fry the fish until just cooked through, then flake it and saute briefly with the onion, tomatoes, and bell pepper.
Mix the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, and water gradually into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Flatten portions of dough into small rounds about 1/2 inch thick.
Heat the oil in a skillet and fry the rounds 2-3 minutes per side until puffed, golden, and cooked through.
Serve the warm bakes split open and filled with the sauteed saltfish mixture.

name: Guyanese Roti and Channa

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps: Saute the curry powder briefly in the oil, then add the chickpeas with a splash of water and simmer 10 minutes.
Make a simple flatbread dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, then cook in a dry, hot skillet until lightly charred and puffed in spots.
Serve the curried chickpeas wrapped in the warm roti.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and ginger in the oil for 4-5 minutes until soft, then stir in the curry powder and cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the chickpeas with 1/2 cup water and simmer 12-15 minutes, mashing a few of the chickpeas to thicken the curry slightly.
Make a simple dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and warm water, kneading until smooth. Rest 15 minutes, then roll thin.

Cook the flatbread in a dry, hot skillet 1-2 minutes per side until lightly charred in spots, brushing with a little butter and folding/slapping it between your hands to flake the layers (the traditional roti technique).
Serve the curried channa wrapped in or alongside the warm roti.

name: Guyanese Egg Ball
steps: Mash the boiled potato and mold it around each hard-boiled egg to fully encase it.
Roll lightly in flour.
Fry in the oil, turning often, until golden all over, about 5-6 minutes.
steps_detailed: Mash the boiled potato with the cumin and black pepper until smooth and well seasoned.
Take a portion of the mashed potato, flatten it in your palm, and mold it around a peeled hard-boiled egg, fully encasing it with an even layer.
Roll each coated egg lightly in the flour.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the balls, turning frequently, 5-7 minutes until golden brown all over and heated through.
Drain briefly and serve warm or at room temperature - a popular Guyanese snack and breakfast item.

name: Guyanese Cook-up Rice
steps: Brown the chicken, then add the beans and coconut milk with a cup of water and bring to a simmer.
Stir in the rice, cover, and cook 18-20 minutes until the rice is tender and has absorbed the coconut broth.
Fluff and serve.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken and brown it with the onion and garlic over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the kidney beans, coconut milk, oregano, black pepper, and 1 cup water. Bring to a simmer.
Stir in the rice, cover, and cook over low heat 18-22 minutes until the rice is tender and has absorbed most of the liquid, stirring once partway through.
Let it rest covered 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork before serving - a one-pot Guyanese staple.

name: Guyanese Dhal and Rice
steps: Simmer the split peas in 2 cups water until very soft, about 25-30 minutes, then mash smooth.
Saute the onion with the curry powder and stir into the mashed peas.
Serve the dhal over the cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the split peas in 2.5 cups water for 25-30 minutes until completely soft, then blend or mash until smooth.
Saute the onion and garlic in the oil with the curry powder and cumin for 4-5 minutes until fragrant.
Stir the sauteed aromatics into the mashed split peas and simmer 5 more minutes, thinning with a little water if too thick.
Stir in the cilantro and serve the dhal ladled over the cooked rice.

Dhal and rice reflects Guyana's large Indo-Guyanese population directly - this dish, and the rice tradition behind it, arrived with 19th-century indentured laborers from India and stayed a genuine part of Guyanese home cooking ever since; basmati is the same rice that tradition was built on.

name: Guyanese Curry Chicken and Roti
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion and curry powder.
Add the potato and a cup of water, cover, and simmer 25 minutes until the chicken and potato are tender.
Serve with roti or rice.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and ginger in the oil for 4-5 minutes, then add the curry powder and cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the chicken and brown it in the curry paste, 5-6 minutes.
Add the potato and 1.25 cups water, cover, and simmer over low heat 25-30 minutes until the chicken and potato are tender.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with warm roti or rice.

name: Guyanese Chow Mein

ingredients.ingredient: Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps: Stir-fry the chicken until cooked through, then remove and set aside.
Stir-fry the cabbage briefly, then add the noodles and soy sauce, tossing to combine.
Return the chicken and toss everything together over high heat 2-3 minutes.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps_detailed: Heat the oil in a wok or large skillet over high heat and stir-fry the chicken until just cooked through, 3-4 minutes. Remove and set aside.
Add the cabbage, carrot, bell pepper, and garlic to the same pan and stir-fry 3-4 minutes until just softened but still with some crunch.
Add the noodles and soy sauce, tossing everything together over high heat for 2-3 minutes until well combined and heated through.

Return the chicken to the pan and toss once more before serving - Guyanese chow mein reflects the country's significant Chinese-Guyanese community and is a beloved local staple, distinct from Chinese-American versions.

name: Guyanese Pepperpot

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Combine the beef, cassareep, brown sugar, and onion in a pot with 1.5 cups water.
Simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce is dark and thick.
Serve hot with bread or rice - traditionally simmered for days, this is the everyday version.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

ingredients_full.note: whole, for warmth, removed before serving

steps_detailed: Combine the beef, cassareep, brown sugar, onion, garlic, whole jalapeno, and bay leaf in a heavy pot with 2 cups water.

Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over low heat 1.5-2.5 hours, stirring occasionally, until the beef is completely tender and falling apart, and the sauce has reduced to a thick, dark, glossy gravy.
Remove the whole jalapeno and bay leaf before serving (they've done their job flavoring the pot without making it too hot).
Serve hot with fresh bread or rice - pepperpot is Guyana's national dish, traditionally made for Christmas and kept going for days, with more cassareep and meat added as it's eaten down.

name: Guyanese Metemgee
steps: Simmer the chicken in water 15 minutes.
Add the cassava, plantain, and coconut milk, and simmer 15-18 minutes until the vegetables are tender.
Season and serve hot.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the onion in 1.5 cups water for 15-18 minutes.
Add the cassava and sweet potato, and simmer 12-15 minutes.
Add the plantain and coconut milk, and continue simmering 10-12 minutes until all the vegetables are tender and the broth has thickened slightly.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot in bowls - a hearty one-pot ground provisions stew.

name: Guyanese Garlic Pork
steps: Marinate the pork in the garlic and vinegar for at least 20 minutes, or overnight for best flavor.
Fry the marinated pork in the oil until well browned and cooked through, about 10-12 minutes.
Serve hot with bread.
steps_detailed: Combine the pork with the garlic, vinegar, bay leaf, and black pepper in a bowl, and marinate at least 30 minutes, or ideally overnight in the fridge - traditionally this dish is marinated for days, developing a distinctive tang.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry the marinated pork, along with its marinade, 10-14 minutes until well browned and cooked through, letting the liquid reduce down.
Remove the bay leaf and serve hot with fresh bread - a Guyanese Christmas tradition with Portuguese roots.

name: Guyanese Baigan Choka

ingredients.ingredient: Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, with salt

steps: Mash the cooked eggplant roughly with a fork.
Saute the onion and garlic in the oil until soft, then stir into the mashed eggplant.
Season well and serve alongside roti or rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, with salt

steps_detailed: Traditionally the eggplant is roasted whole over an open flame until the skin blackens and the inside is smoky and soft - simmering or boiling until very tender is a good stand-in if you don't have a grill.
Mash the cooked eggplant roughly with a fork, leaving some texture.
Saute the onion, garlic, and jalapeno in the oil for 4-5 minutes until soft, then stir in the diced tomatoes and cook 2-3 minutes.
Fold the sauteed mixture into the mashed eggplant and season well with salt.
Stir in the cilantro and serve warm or at room temperature alongside roti or rice.

name: Guyanese Pholourie

ingredients.note: soaked and blended, or use split pea flour

steps: Blend the soaked split peas with the flour, curry powder, and a little water into a thick batter.
Drop spoonfuls into the hot oil and fry until puffed and golden, turning occasionally.
Serve with a tangy dipping sauce.

ingredients_full.note: soaked and blended, or use split pea flour

steps_detailed: Soak the split peas in water for at least 4 hours or overnight, then drain and blend with a little fresh water into a smooth, thick batter.
Stir in the flour, curry powder, baking powder, and garlic until well combined.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat. Drop rounded spoonfuls of the batter into the hot oil and fry, turning occasionally, 3-4 minutes until puffed, golden, and cooked through.
Drain on a paper towel and serve with a tangy tamarind or pepper sauce for dipping.

name: Guyanese Bara
steps: Blend the soaked split peas into a smooth paste, then mix with the flour and cumin into a thick batter.
Flatten spoonfuls slightly and fry in the oil until golden, about 2 minutes per side.
Serve warm, often alongside curried channa.
steps_detailed: Soak the split peas for at least 4 hours, drain, and blend into a smooth, thick paste with minimal water.
Mix the pea paste with the flour, cumin, baking powder, and turmeric until you have a thick, spreadable batter.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat. Drop spoonfuls of batter into the oil, flattening slightly with the back of the spoon.
Fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown and cooked through.
Serve warm, traditionally alongside channa or a tamarind dipping sauce.

name: Guyanese Pine Tart

ingredients.ingredient: Pineapple, canned, juice pack, solids and liquids

steps: Make a simple pastry dough from the flour and butter with a pinch of salt and a little cold water.
Simmer the chopped pineapple with the sugar for 8-10 minutes until thick and jammy.
Roll out the dough, fill with the pineapple jam, fold over, and bake at 375F for 18-20 minutes until golden.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Pineapple, canned, juice pack, solids and liquids

steps_detailed: Rub the butter into the flour with a pinch of salt until crumbly, then add just enough cold water to bring it into a smooth dough. Chill 20 minutes.
Simmer the chopped pineapple with the sugar and cinnamon for 10-12 minutes until thick, jammy, and most of the liquid has cooked off.
Roll the dough thin and cut into squares or rounds, spoon the pineapple filling onto one half of each, fold over, and crimp the edges to seal.
Brush with the beaten egg and bake at 375F (190C) for 18-22 minutes until golden brown.
Cool slightly before eating - the filling stays hot.

name: Guyanese Fried Plantain
steps: Slice the ripe plantain on the diagonal.
Fry in the oil 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown and caramelized at the edges.
Drain briefly and serve warm.
steps_detailed: Slice the ripe (yellow to black-speckled) plantain on the diagonal into pieces about 1/2 inch thick.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the plantain slices 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and caramelized at the edges.
Drain briefly on a paper towel and dust with a little cinnamon if you like.
Serve warm as a simple, sweet side or snack - the riper the plantain, the sweeter the result.

name: Guyanese Cassava Pone
steps: Mix the grated cassava with the coconut milk and brown sugar.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish.
Bake at 350F for 40-45 minutes until firm and golden brown on top.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Mix the grated cassava thoroughly with the coconut milk, brown sugar, cinnamon, and raisins.
Pour into a small, well-buttered baking dish and press down evenly.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 45-50 minutes until firm throughout, deeply golden, and slightly caramelized on top.
Let cool at least 20 minutes before slicing into squares - it firms up considerably as it cools.

name: Guyanese Black Cake

ingredients.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Cream the butter and brown sugar until dark and well combined.
Fold in the flour and the soaked raisins.
Bake at 325F for 35-40 minutes until deeply colored and a toothpick comes out mostly clean.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Soak the raisins in a little fruit juice or water overnight (traditionally rum and wine, for a special-occasion version) until plump.
Cream the butter and brown sugar together until dark and well combined, then beat in the egg and vanilla.
Sift together the flour, baking powder, and cinnamon and fold into the batter along with the soaked raisins and their soaking liquid.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish and bake at 325F (165C) for 40-45 minutes until deeply colored and a toothpick comes out mostly clean.
Traditionally made weeks ahead for Christmas and fed with more rum as it ages - this is a simpler, everyday version of the same rich, dark cake.

name: Guyanese Sweet Bread
steps: Mix the flour, coconut, sugar, and softened butter with a splash of milk into a soft dough.
Shape into a small loaf.
Bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
steps_detailed: Combine the flour, shredded coconut, sugar, baking powder, and cinnamon in a bowl.
Rub in the softened butter until the mixture looks like coarse crumbs, then stir in the milk and vanilla to form a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Shape into a small loaf and place in a buttered loaf pan.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 35-40 minutes until golden brown and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Let cool slightly before slicing - dense, sweet, and coconut-studded, a Guyanese bakery staple.

name: Guyanese Achar
steps: Toss the cabbage and carrot with the vinegar and curry powder.
Let sit at least 30 minutes, or overnight in the fridge, to pickle.
Serve as a tangy side with curries or fried snacks.
steps_detailed: Combine the shredded cabbage and carrot with the garlic and jalapeno.
Toss with the vinegar and curry powder until everything is well coated.
Let sit at room temperature at least 30 minutes, or refrigerate overnight for a more deeply pickled flavor.
Serve as a tangy, crunchy condiment alongside curries, roti, or fried snacks.

name: Guyanese Pepper Sauce
steps: Blend the peppers, vinegar, and garlic into a smooth, fiery sauce.
Season with salt.
Use sparingly - this is meant to be very hot.
steps_detailed: Combine the peppers, vinegar, garlic, onion, and lime juice in a blender and blend until smooth.
Season with salt to taste.
Store in a sealed jar in the fridge - a little goes a long way, and it keeps for weeks.
